summary: Gruber examines Apple's latest DMA compliance changes for the EU, arguing that the European Commission's mandates — including a revised browser choice screen, deletable system apps, and default app settings — are technically ignorant, counterproductive, and ultimately harmful to users. He contends the browser choice screen will most benefit Google Chrome, the very monopoly the DMA should target, while punishing Safari users who genuinely prefer it. He argues the requirement to delete core apps like Photos and Camera is performative regulation that ignores how iOS is actually architected. Most significantly, he makes the case that the DMA's fundamental hostility to integration — Apple's core design philosophy — explains why Apple Intelligence and iPhone Mirroring won't launch in the EU, and predicts this pattern will repeat for every future integrated Apple product or service.
key_points: The revised EU browser choice screen repeatedly forces Safari users to re-choose while exempting users of other defaults, and will likely benefit Chrome — the world's dominant browser monopoly — more than any smaller competitor.
Requiring core apps like Photos, Camera, and Messages to be deletable is performative regulation that ignores iOS's non-modular architecture, since the underlying system frameworks remain unchanged.
DMA supporters often conflate the law with App Store opposition, but the DMA is actually a sweeping attack on the entire concept of integration, which is Apple's fundamental design philosophy.

Apple Intelligence and iPhone Mirroring aren't being withheld from the EU out of spite — they genuinely raise DMA compliance issues because the law targets any integration that could preference Apple's own products.
The long-term effect of the DMA will be that new Apple products and services come late, or never, to the EU — the opposite of what the regulation intended.
money_quotes: At what point do these restrictions punish Safari users who want to use Safari? I'd say the EC has crossed that point by forcing these rules on Apple.
This is legally-mandated bad interaction design.

My guess is that, perhaps counterintuitively, the single biggest beneficiary of this mandatory browser choice screen will be Google Chrome, which I consider the single most dominant software monopoly in the world today.
The key is that the DMA is not a targeted attack on the App Store model. It's a sweepingly broad attack on the entire idea of integration. And integration is Apple's entire modus operandi.
This is performative regulation. None of this deletable apps nonsense increases competition; it merely increases the chances of profound user confusion.
The European Commission is beset by delusions of grandeur.
While not the intention, that's the actual effect of the DMA.
key_insight: The DMA's fundamental hostility to integration — not just the App Store — means every future Apple product that ties hardware, software, and services together will arrive late or never in the EU, making the regulation's actual effect the opposite of its stated intent.
